facts.text: The first Winter Olympics were held in Chamonix, France, in 1924.
The Winter Games feature cold-weather sports such as skiing, snowboarding, ice hockey, curling, and figure skating.
Figure skating was actually contested at the Olympics before the first official Winter Games existed.
Norway has historically been one of the most successful nations in Winter Olympic medal counts.
The Winter Olympics were originally held in the same year as the Summer Games before shifting to a staggered two-year cycle in the 1990s.
The Miracle on Ice at the 1980 Winter Olympics remains one of the most famous moments in sports history.
Alpine skiing includes downhill, slalom, giant slalom, super-G, and combined events.
Snowboarding joined the Winter Olympics in 1998 and quickly became one of the Games' most popular additions.
Curling, sometimes called 'chess on ice,' has become a fan favorite because of its strategy and precision.
Biathlon combines cross-country skiing with rifle shooting, making it one of the Winter Games' most unusual events.
The Winter Olympics showcase some of the fastest non-motorized sports on Earth, including luge, skeleton, and bobsled.
Yuna Kim, Lindsey Vonn, Shaun White, Mikaela Shiffrin, and Ole Einar Bjørndalen are among the iconic modern Winter Olympians.
The 1994 Lillehammer Olympics are widely remembered as one of the most successful and beloved Winter Games ever hosted.
Cold weather, altitude, and snow conditions can dramatically affect performance and even which athletes are favored.
The Winter Olympics have helped popularize sports that many people would otherwise only see rarely on television.
From blades to skis to sled runners, Winter Olympic equipment is heavily optimized for speed, aerodynamics, and control.
The Winter Olympic Games became the leading global showcase for snow and ice sports.
Its events helped define many of the world's most watched winter competitions.
